medications.indication_rationale: Lubricates the mouth before/at meals so food slides without adhering; the mainstay of symptom relief.
Soothes mucosal irritation; avoid alcohol-based rinses which worsen dryness.
A rheumatology/GP decision for ongoing sicca management, not an acute telehealth initiation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Spray artificial saliva 5 minutes before meals, avoid dry/crumbly foods (crackers/toast), sip water with meals, and chew sugar-free gum to stimulate saliva.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents food adhering to dry mucosa and eases dysphagia.

patient_education_points.point: Your Sjogren's means too little saliva, so dry crackers stuck to your mouth and throat and burned — but you can swallow water and your saliva and your voice is normal, so nothing is actually blocking your throat.
Use artificial saliva before meals, avoid dry/crumbly foods, sip water with food, and chew sugar-free gum. See your GP/rheumatologist about a saliva-stimulating medication.

Go to ED if you become unable to swallow your own saliva, start drooling, your voice changes or you get noisy breathing, or if you develop a fever with painful swelling in front of your ear or under your jaw.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Sicca management and secretagogues; monitor for candidiasis.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Inability to swallow saliva/drooling (bolus), or fever with glandular swelling (sialadenitis).

must_include_items: Exclude food-bolus impaction (swallows water/saliva, normal voice, no drooling) and parotitis (fever/glandular swelling)
Advise artificial saliva before meals and avoiding dry foods
Reassure the airway/throat is not blocked
errors_of_commission: Ambulance/ED over-triage for a patient swallowing water with a normal voice
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T1/T2 case: passing requires the bolus/parotitis screen + lubrication advice + safety-net. ED over-triage of friction dysphagia with intact swallow/voice is a failure.
